d: Bone separates at a growth plate
e: A comminuted fracture occurs when high-energy force — such as a motor vehicle collision or gunshot wound — shatters bone into three or more fragments. Because the bone is broken into multiple pieces, surgical repair with plates and screws is often required to restore alignment and stability.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added bone twists and bone collapses as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact explanation with clinical educational content.

d: Weakening of jaw bone without tooth loss
e: Complete edentulism — total loss of all permanent teeth — affects approximately 10% of older adults worldwide. It results from severe tooth decay, periodontal disease, or trauma. Treatment typically involves dentures or dental implants to restore chewing function, speech, and facial structure.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; clarified distractor phrasing. Replaced shark fun-fact explanation with clinical educational content.

d: Complete inability to turn the head
Limited neck range of motion in all directions
e: Congenital torticollis occurs when the sternocleidomastoid muscle — which runs from behind the ear to the collarbone — is shortened or fibrotic at birth, pulling the head to one side. It is often caused by birth trauma or abnormal positioning in the womb. Physical therapy stretching exercises are the primary treatment and are effective in most cases when started early.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added shoulder elevation and limited neck range as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) tests detect SARS-CoV-2 by amplifying the virus's genetic material from a nasal swab sample. They are highly sensitive and specific. Rapid antigen tests detect viral proteins and give results in minutes but are less sensitive than PCR. Both test types became essential public health tools during the COVID-19 pandemic.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added metapneumovirus and coronavirus NL63 as plausible alternative respiratory viruses. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

d: Close the vocal cords for intubation
i: Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science (this is a clinical procedure technique). Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added occlude airway and external cardiac compression as plausible wrong answers.

e: Dental pulp exposure occurs when decay, fracture, or dental work removes enough tooth structure to expose the central pulp chamber containing nerves and blood vessels. Exposed pulp is exquisitely sensitive and at high risk of bacterial infection. Treatment typically requires root canal therapy to remove the pulp and seal the tooth.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added alveolar bone and crown surface as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: A depressed skull fracture occurs when blunt force drives bone fragments inward toward the brain. The displaced bone can lacerate the dura mater (protective membrane), damage brain tissue, or compress underlying blood vessels. Surgical elevation and repair are needed when the depression exceeds the skull's thickness or when neurological symptoms are present.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added along suture lines and toward opposite hemisphere as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: Diffuse brain injury — most commonly from rapid acceleration-deceleration (such as in car crashes) — damages axons throughout the brain rather than in one spot. This is called diffuse axonal injury (DAI) and disrupts communication between brain regions. It is a major cause of persistent vegetative states and long-term cognitive impairment after trauma.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added temporal lobe exclusively and localized contusion site as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.
Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added endorphin and oxytocin as plausible neurotransmitter/signaling molecule alternatives.

Fixed l2 from anatomy_organs to medical_science (this is about a medical treatment protocol). Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added joint traction and debridement as plausible wrong answers. Note: the question asks what 'C' stands for in RICE — distractors correctly include non-C components.

e: Tendinopathy refers to degeneration and pain in a tendon, often from repetitive overuse rather than acute inflammation. In the elbow, lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) involves the extensor tendons of the forearm, while medial epicondylitis (golfer's elbow) involves the flexor tendons. Treatment includes rest, eccentric exercises, and physical therapy.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; made distractors more anatomically specific. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: Endarteritis is inflammation of the tunica intima — the innermost layer of an artery lining the lumen. It can be caused by infection (as in tertiary syphilis), autoimmune disease, or prosthetic graft complications. Unlike atherosclerosis, which involves lipid buildup, endarteritis involves inflammatory cell infiltration of the arterial lining.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; made anatomically precise. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

d: Treating bile duct infections with antibiotics
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added sealing esophageal perforations and dilating a vessel surgically as plausible wrong answers.
Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added pituitary gland and hypothalamus as plausible hormone-producing organ alternatives.

e: Esophageal dilation is performed endoscopically using balloon dilators or bougie (graduated) dilators passed through the mouth. It treats strictures caused by acid reflux scarring, eosinophilic esophagitis, or prior surgery. Multiple sessions may be required for severely narrowed sections.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added cardiac sphincter and gastric antrum as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

The patient is restricted to liquid diet only
A jejunal free flap replaces the segment

d: Broken bone only, no joint involvement
Muscle tear combined with a fracture
Ligament rupture combined with a fracture
Nerve damage combined with joint injury
Two separate fractures at the same site
Cartilage damage combined with bone bruising
e: A fracture dislocation is a severe injury where a bone is both broken and displaced from its joint. It most commonly occurs at the ankle, shoulder, hip, or spine following high-energy trauma. These injuries are surgical emergencies because displaced bone fragments can damage nerves and blood vessels.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added cartilage damage and tendon avulsion as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: Frostbite occurs when tissue temperature drops below 0°C (32°F), causing ice crystals to form inside cells and in extracellular fluid. This ruptures cell membranes and obstructs blood flow. Superficial frostbite (frostnip) affects only the skin, while deep frostbite penetrates to muscle and bone and can require amputation.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added hypothermia without freezing and cold water immersion as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

d: Vivid imagery occurring only during sleep
Seeing complex images only with closed eyes
Temporary visual disturbances after eye strain
e: Hallucinosis is characterized by persistent hallucinations — auditory, visual, or tactile — that occur in a person who is fully conscious and aware. Unlike delirium, the individual often recognizes the experiences as abnormal. It can be caused by alcohol withdrawal, stimulant use, or neurological conditions such as Parkinson's disease.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added complete disconnection from reality and recurring intrusive memories as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

q: What is an acquired hand deformity, as distinct from a congenital one?
a: A structural change to the hand that develops after birth from injury, disease, or overuse
d: A hand abnormality present at birth due to genetic factors
A temporary hand weakness from nerve compression
A hereditary condition affecting hand bones
A transient swelling from acute inflammation
A cosmetic variation with no functional impact
A hand deformity caused only by chromosomal disorders
Any deformity involving only the fingernails
A condition exclusive to childhood development
e: Unlike congenital hand deformities that arise during fetal development, acquired deformities develop after birth. Causes include fractures healing in misalignment, rheumatoid arthritis eroding joints, tendon injuries, burns causing contractures, or nerve damage altering muscle balance. Treatment depends on the underlying cause and degree of functional impairment.
i: Rewrote question from off-topic bone-count trivia to a clinically relevant question about what distinguishes acquired from congenital hand deformity. Expanded distractors to 8. Updated explanation to be educational.

e: The head-tilt chin-lift maneuver extends the neck and lifts the mandible, moving the tongue away from the posterior pharynx to open the airway. It is the first step in basic life support when no cervical spine injury is suspected. If spinal injury is a concern, the jaw-thrust maneuver is used instead.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added prevents spinal movement and clears oropharynx as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: Medical heavy metal detoxification uses chelation therapy — drugs such as DMSA, EDTA, or BAL that bind metal ions (lead, mercury, arsenic, cadmium) in the blood and tissues and carry them out through urine. It is used for confirmed poisoning. The alternative medicine use of chelation therapy for conditions other than proven heavy metal toxicity is not supported by clinical evidence.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added metabolic waste products and environmental allergens as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: Hypophosphatemic rickets is a rare inherited condition where the kidneys waste phosphate in the urine, leaving bones unable to mineralize properly. Unlike vitamin D deficiency rickets, supplementing vitamin D alone does not correct it. The most common form is X-linked (caused by PHEX gene mutations) and is treated with oral phosphate supplements and active vitamin D.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added copper and selenium as plausible mineral alternatives. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: An insulin coma (hypoglycemic coma) occurs when blood glucose falls critically low — typically below 2 mmol/L — depriving the brain of fuel. It can result from excess insulin administration, skipped meals, or strenuous exercise in a diabetic patient. Emergency treatment is intravenous dextrose (glucose) or intramuscular glucagon.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added diabetic ketoacidosis and hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.
Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added complement system activation and neutrophil recruitment as plausible immune response alternatives.

d: Abnormal dilation of the bowel lumen
e: An intestinal fistula is an abnormal tract connecting the intestine to another organ or the skin surface. Types include enterocutaneous (intestine to skin), enteroenteric (between intestinal loops), and rectovaginal fistulas. Causes include Crohn's disease, post-surgical complications, or radiation injury. Many require surgical repair.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; made anatomically specific. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

d: Sterile water injections for back pain
e: The Lamaze technique teaches pregnant women controlled breathing patterns — typically rhythmic breathing that focuses attention and promotes relaxation during contractions. Developed by French obstetrician Fernand Lamaze in the 1950s based on Pavlovian conditioning principles, it is now taught in childbirth education classes worldwide and can reduce the perception of labor pain.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added TENS and sterile water injections as plausible pain-management alternatives. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: Lead interferes with neural development by substituting for calcium in neuronal processes, disrupting synaptic transmission and myelination. In children, even low blood lead levels cause irreversible IQ reduction, attention disorders, and behavioral problems. In adults, lead toxicity causes peripheral neuropathy, cognitive decline, and mood disturbances.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added reproductive system and renal system as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

Seven sounds spanning narrow frequency range
Three sounds for gross hearing detection
Nine sounds testing pitch discrimination only
Ten phonemes for speech recognition testing
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added ten phonemes and two sounds as plausible wrong numerical answers.

d: Pleural fluid from the chest cavity
e: A lumbar puncture (spinal tap) is performed by inserting a needle between lumbar vertebrae (usually L3-L4 or L4-L5) into the subarachnoid space to collect cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). CSF analysis is used to diagnose meningitis, subarachnoid hemorrhage, multiple sclerosis, and Guillain-Barré syndrome. The procedure can also measure intracranial pressure.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added pleural fluid and peritoneal fluid as plausible body fluid alternatives. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

d: Broken ends remain separated without bridging
Bone resorbs and shortens at the fracture site
Cartilage grows at the fracture site (pseudarthrosis)
e: A malunion occurs when fracture ends heal with residual angulation, rotation, or shortening. This can cause limb length discrepancy, joint malalignment, and abnormal gait. Corrective osteotomy — surgically rebreaking and realigning the bone — is sometimes required to restore function and prevent premature arthritis in adjacent joints.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added pseudarthrosis and post-traumatic arthritis as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: Mandibular fractures are among the most common facial fractures, often caused by assault, falls, or motor vehicle crashes. The mandible (lower jaw) has several common fracture sites: the condyle, angle, body, and symphysis. Fractures may cause malocclusion (teeth no longer meeting properly), pain, and difficulty swallowing. Treatment may require wiring the jaws together or surgical fixation.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added hyoid bone and sphenoid bone as plausible facial bone alternatives. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: The maxilla forms the middle third of the face and contains the upper teeth. Maxillary fractures — often classified by Le Fort levels (I, II, III) — can destabilize the entire mid-face. High-energy trauma such as motor vehicle crashes or industrial accidents is the most common cause. Surgical fixation restores facial support and proper dental occlusion.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added palatine bone and pterygoid plates as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

e: Congenital mouth abnormalities — including cleft lip, cleft palate, and tongue malformations — develop during the first trimester when oral structures fuse. Cleft lip and palate affect approximately 1 in 700 births globally and require coordinated surgical repair, speech therapy, and orthodontic treatment over many years.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added nutritional deficiencies and dental trauma as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

d: Nerves become acutely inflamed and swollen
Nerves swell from edematous fluid accumulation
Nerves lose myelin without axon damage
e: Nerve degeneration (neurodegeneration) involves progressive loss of neuronal structure and function. Axon degeneration following injury (Wallerian degeneration) proceeds distal to the site of injury. Diseases such as ALS, multiple sclerosis, and peripheral neuropathy involve degeneration of motor or sensory neurons, causing weakness, numbness, and loss of autonomic function.

d: Permanent total blindness only (no partial loss possible)
Enhanced color perception from neural compensation
e: Optic nerve injury can range from partial visual field loss to complete blindness depending on severity and location. Traumatic optic neuropathy occurs when fractures or swelling compress the nerve in its bony canal. Prompt treatment with corticosteroids or surgical decompression may preserve vision if started early.

e: Oral hemorrhage can arise from tooth extraction sockets, gingival bleeding from periodontal disease, soft tissue lacerations, or oral cancers. It can also be a sign of systemic bleeding disorders such as hemophilia or thrombocytopenia. Management depends on identifying the source: local pressure, suturing, or treating the underlying coagulopathy.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added mucosal ulceration and salivary gland tumor as plausible wrong answers. Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.

d: The cerebral cortex and brain parenchyma
The hepatic tissue and bile ducts
e: Ototoxic substances damage the cochlea (hearing) and vestibular system (balance) in the inner ear. Common ototoxic medications include aminoglycoside antibiotics (gentamicin, tobramycin), platinum chemotherapy agents (cisplatin), and loop diuretics (furosemide at high doses). The cochlear hair cells — once destroyed — do not regenerate in humans, making the hearing loss permanent.
i: Expanded distractors from 6 to 8; added vestibular apparatus only and facial nerve as plausible wrong answers (the vestibular system is also affected but the question asks what ototoxicity damages, cochlea/ear is the answer). Replaced fun-fact with clinical explanation.
